title: [InstCombine] Handle trunc to i1 in align assume.

tests.source_program:
target datalayout = "e-m:e-i64:64-f80:128-n8:16:32:64-S128"
target triple = "x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu"

; Function Attrs: nocallback nofree nosync nounwind willreturn memory(inaccessiblemem: write)
declare void @llvm.assume(i1 noundef) #0

; Function Attrs: nounwind uwtable
define i32 @trunc_with_pointer_offset(ptr %a) #1 {
  %t0 = load i32, ptr %a, align 4
  %ptrint = ptrtoint ptr %a to i64
  %offset = add i64 %ptrint, 4
  %trunc = trunc i64 %offset to i1
  %maskcond = xor i1 %trunc, true
  tail call void @llvm.assume(i1 %maskcond)
  ret i32 %t0
}
tests.expect_optimized_program:
target datalayout = "e-m:e-i64:64-f80:128-n8:16:32:64-S128"
target triple = "x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu"

; Function Attrs: nocallback nofree nosync nounwind willreturn memory(inaccessiblemem: write)
declare void @llvm.assume(i1 noundef) #0

; Function Attrs: nounwind uwtable
define i32 @trunc_with_pointer_offset(ptr %a) #1 {
  %t0 = load i32, ptr %a, align 4
  tail call void @llvm.assume(i1 true) ["align"(ptr %a, i64 2)]
  ret i32 %t0
}
tests.outside_scope_reason: Summary doesn't mention that the value A can include offset adjustments (match(A, m_Add(...))). The code extracts Offset from add operations before trunc, which is an undocumented capability.

tests.source_program:
target datalayout = "e-m:e-i64:64-f80:128-n8:16:32:64-S128"
target triple = "x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu"

declare void @llvm.assume(i1 noundef)

define i32 @trunc_with_icmp_ne_negation(ptr %a) {
  %t0 = load i32, ptr %a, align 4
  %ptrint = ptrtoint ptr %a to i64
  %trunc = trunc i64 %ptrint to i1
  %maskcond = icmp ne i1 %trunc, false
  tail call void @llvm.assume(i1 %maskcond)
  ret i32 %t0
}
tests.expect_optimized_program:
target datalayout = "e-m:e-i64:64-f80:128-n8:16:32:64-S128"
target triple = "x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu"

declare void @llvm.assume(i1 noundef)

define i32 @trunc_with_icmp_ne_negation(ptr %a) {
  %t0 = load i32, ptr %a, align 4
  tail call void @llvm.assume(i1 true) ["align"(ptr %a, i64 2)]
  ret i32 %t0
}
tests.outside_scope_reason: Summary describes not(trunc(A)) pattern but existing test uses xor with true. This uses icmp ne instruction for negation instead, which is semantically equivalent but a different IR representation not mentioned in the summary.

tests.source_program:
target datalayout = "e-m:e-i64:64-f80:128-n8:16:32:64-S128"
target triple = "x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu"

declare void @llvm.assume(i1 noundef)

define i32 @trunc_with_pointer_offset(ptr %a) {
  %t0 = load i32, ptr %a, align 4
  %ptrint = ptrtoint ptr %a to i64
  %offset = add i64 %ptrint, 1
  %trunc = trunc i64 %offset to i1
  %maskcond = xor i1 %trunc, true
  tail call void @llvm.assume(i1 %maskcond)
  ret i32 %t0
}
tests.expect_optimized_program:
target datalayout = "e-m:e-i64:64-f80:128-n8:16:32:64-S128"
target triple = "x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu"

declare void @llvm.assume(i1 noundef)

define i32 @trunc_with_pointer_offset(ptr %a) {
  %t0 = load i32, ptr %a, align 4
  tail call void @llvm.assume(i1 true) ["align"(ptr %a, i64 2)]
  ret i32 %t0
}
tests.outside_scope_reason: Summary describes trunc pattern on raw pointer integer but doesn't mention pointer arithmetic before trunc. This adds an offset via add instruction before trunc, testing the m_Add pattern matching in the code that extracts Offset which isn't described in the summary.
